title: Medicare 101: What Is Medicare Plan B? A Clear Guide for 2026
rawContent:
[Generated from outline]
Keyword: Medicare 101: What Is Medicare Plan B?
Content Type: blog

sections.text: Introduction: Making Sense of Medicare Part B

sections.content: A single letter can carry a lot of weight when you are new to Medicare. Part A, Part B, Part D... it can start to feel like alphabet soup rather than a health insurance plan. Part B tends to cause the most head-scratching because it covers so much of your everyday medical care, yet its rules around costs and timing are often misunderstood.

This guide breaks down Part B in plain language so you can move forward with a clear picture rather than guesswork. You will learn what it covers, what it costs in 2026, when to enroll, and how it fits together with the rest of your Medicare choices. By the end, you should feel ready to compare your options instead of feeling stuck.

sections.text: What Medicare Part B Actually Covers

sections.content: Medicare Part B is medical insurance. It covers the care you receive outside of a hospital stay, including doctor visits, outpatient procedures, and preventive services designed to catch problems early.
Part B pays for a wide range of everyday healthcare needs. Some of the most commonly used services include:

items: Annual wellness visits and routine checkups
Durable medical equipment, such as wheelchairs or walkers

sections.calloutText: Part B does not cover hospital stays, most prescription drugs, or routine dental, vision, and hearing care. Those needs typically require Part A, a Part D drug plan, or separate supplemental coverage.

sections.text: Part B vs. Part A: Understanding the Difference

sections.content: Original Medicare is made up of two main parts that work together. Part A covers inpatient care, meaning services you receive after being formally admitted to a hospital or skilled nursing facility. Part B covers outpatient and medical care, the kind you receive without an overnight hospital stay.

Think of it this way: a visit to your primary care doctor for a check-up falls under Part B. If that same visit leads to a hospital admission for surgery, Part A takes over for the inpatient portion of your care. Together, these two parts form the foundation of Original Medicare.

sections.text: What Medicare Part B Costs in 2026

sections.content: Understanding the cost structure of Part B upfront can help you avoid surprises once you start using your coverage. In 2026, most people pay a standard monthly premium of $202.90, though your amount could be higher depending on your income.

If your income is above certain thresholds, you may pay an Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount, known as IRMAA. For example, an individual earning more than $109,000 but less than $137,000 in 2024 (the look-back year used for 2026 premiums) would pay $284.10 per month instead of the standard rate. Higher income brackets scale up from there, reaching $689.90 monthly for the highest earners.

sections.calloutText: Knowing your premium tier before you enroll helps you budget accurately. If your income has changed recently due to retirement, you may be able to request an adjustment to your IRMAA determination through the Social Security Administration.

sections.text: How to Budget for Out-of-Pocket Expenses

sections.content: Once you meet your annual Part B deductible of $283, Medicare generally pays 80% of the approved cost for covered services. You are responsible for the remaining 20%, known as coinsurance, and this cost-sharing continues for the rest of the year with no annual cap under Original Medicare alone.

This is where many people choose to add supplemental coverage. A [Medicare Supplement plan](/medicare-supplement-plans/plan-g/), often called Medigap, can help cover some or most of that 20% coinsurance, reducing the risk of unpredictable medical bills.

sections.text: Will Your Doctors and Care Needs Be Covered Under Part B?

sections.content: One of the biggest worries when reviewing Medicare is whether your current doctors will still be available to you. Not every provider accepts Medicare assignment, which affects how much you pay and whether your claims are billed directly to Medicare.

The good news is that most doctors nationwide do accept Medicare. You can confirm whether a specific provider participates by asking their office directly or by searching the [Medicare physician compare tool](https://www.medicare.gov/care-compare/) before your appointment.

Part B covers most standard outpatient and preventive care you would expect from routine medical treatment. However, it does not include routine dental, vision, or hearing services, which is a common surprise for new enrollees. If those needs are a priority for you, it may be worth comparing Medicare Advantage plans or standalone coverage that fills those gaps.

sections.calloutText: Routine dental cleanings, eyeglasses, and hearing aids are not covered by Original Medicare Part B. Many people address this gap through a Medicare Advantage plan or a separate ancillary policy.

sections.text: Enrollment Deadlines and How to Avoid a Late Penalty

sections.content: Your Initial Enrollment Period begins three months before the month you turn 65, includes your birthday month, and extends three months after, giving you a seven-month window overall. Enrolling during this window helps you avoid coverage gaps and potential penalties.

If you delay signing up for Part B without other qualifying coverage, such as an employer group health plan, you could face a lifetime late enrollment penalty. This penalty adds 10% to your monthly premium for each full 12-month period you were eligible but did not enroll, and it does not go away once assessed.

sections.calloutText: The Part B late enrollment penalty is permanent for as long as you have Medicare. Confirming your enrollment timeline early can help you avoid this added cost altogether.

sections.content: If you are still working past 65 and covered by a qualifying employer group health plan, you likely qualify for a Special Enrollment Period. This allows you to delay Part B without penalty until your employment or coverage ends.

sections.text: Choosing the Right Time to Enroll Based on Your Situation

sections.content: If retirement is on the horizon, it generally makes sense to enroll in Part B during your Initial Enrollment Period so your coverage begins without a gap. If you plan to keep working past 65 with employer coverage, you may be able to wait, but it is worth confirming with your benefits administrator that your plan qualifies for a Special Enrollment Period.
Either way, checking your coverage status early, rather than waiting until a deadline is close, gives you time to ask questions and make an informed choice.

I always tell people to mark their Initial Enrollment Period on a calendar the moment they know it's coming, even if they're not ready to decide yet. Missing that window by even a month can mean paying a permanent penalty for the rest of your life on Medicare. Give yourself a buffer of a few weeks before the deadline so a lost form or a scheduling delay doesn't cost you later.

sections.text: How Part B Fits Into Your Bigger Medicare Decision

sections.content: Part B is the foundation on which most other Medicare decisions are built. Once you have Part A and Part B in place, you can choose to add a Medigap policy to help cover out-of-pocket costs, or you can choose a Medicare Advantage plan that bundles your hospital, medical, and often drug coverage into one plan.

Rather than focusing only on which option has the lowest monthly premium, it helps to compare based on your doctors, your prescriptions, and the type of care you expect to need. A plan with a lower premium can sometimes cost more overall if it does not align with your health needs.

sections.calloutTitle: You Don't Have to Decide Alone
sections.calloutText: A licensed Medicare agent can walk through your specific doctors, medications, and budget with you, at no cost and with no obligation, so you can compare options clearly before making a decision.

sections.content: Taking the time now to compare your choices carefully can help you avoid switching plans later or discovering a coverage gap after you already need care.

sections.text: Frequently Asked Questions About Medicare Part B

faqs.question: I'm not ready to enroll yet. Can I take more time to decide?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is fine to research at your own pace. Just keep your Initial Enrollment Period dates in mind, since missing that window without other qualifying coverage can lead to a permanent late enrollment penalty.

faqs.question: Will I get a pushy sales call if I ask about Part B coverage?
faqs.answer: No. Licensed Medicare agents are there to explain your options clearly, not to pressure you into a decision. Transparent, no-obligation guidance is the standard you should expect from any trustworthy source.

faqs.question: Can I figure out Part B on my own by reading more?
faqs.answer: Self-research is a great first step, and many people start exactly this way. When you are ready, a licensed agent can confirm details specific to your doctors and prescriptions to make sure nothing gets missed.

faqs.question: How do I know which sources about Medicare I can trust?
faqs.answer: Look for plain-language explanations without heavy sales language, clear transparency about which plans are available in your area, and real client reviews. These signs typically point to a source focused on your needs rather than a quick sale.

sections.content: Medicare Part B covers the outpatient and preventive care you rely on most, from doctor visits to lab tests, and understanding its costs, from the $202.90 standard premium to the 20% coinsurance, helps you plan ahead rather than react to surprise bills.

Before you enroll, take time to compare your specific needs, your doctors, your prescriptions, and your budget. When you are ready for a second opinion, a licensed Medicare agent can offer personalized, no-pressure guidance to help you choose coverage you feel good about.

excerpt: A clear breakdown of Medicare Part B for 2026, covering what it pays for, its costs, and how it fits with the rest of Original Medicare.
keyTakeaways: Medicare Part B covers outpatient and medical care such as doctor visits, preventive services, and outpatient procedures.
In 2026, the standard Part B monthly premium is $202.90, with higher amounts for those subject to IRMAA based on income.
The 2026 Part B annual deductible is $283, after which Medicare typically pays 80% of approved costs, leaving you responsible for the remaining 20% coinsurance.
Part B does not cover hospital stays, most prescription drugs, or routine dental, vision, and hearing care.
Original Medicare has no annual cap on out-of-pocket coinsurance, which is why many people add supplemental coverage.
